storm.summary_layperson: A line of thunderstorms is approaching from about 33 miles to the west and is expected to reach the cove around 5:30–6:20 PM this evening; the cool Long Island Sound often weakens storms coming from that direction before they arrive.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 71°F — 12°F cooler than the air — creates a moderate marine stable layer that tends to choke incoming convection from the west.
storm.detail_html:
A line of showers and thunderstorms tied to an approaching cold front is moving southeast at 38 kt. The lead cell is 33 miles west of the cove at 39 dBZ — solid precipitation, though not yet severe. NWS OKX flags an isolated strong-to-severe wind threat through 9 PM, with the primary focus on NE New Jersey and the NYC metro; a broader isolated threat extends to the CT coast between 4–10 PM. CAPE is low at 770 J/kg, pointing toward active but not deeply unstable convection.

Local conditions are moderating the threat. Long Island Sound water stands at 71°F — 12°F below the air temperature — maintaining a moderate stable marine layer over the water. The storm is approaching from due west, perpendicular to the Sound axis, the geometry most favorable for cooling and weakening as cells cross the open water. Low CAPE (770 J/kg, well below the 1,500 J/kg punch-through threshold) means the convection is easy to choke at low levels. The Sound effect likelihood is rated moderate: the cove should see less intense rain and fewer lightning strikes than Westchester or northern Greenwich.

The lead cell is on a closing track at 21 kt. The arrival window is 5:24–6:20 PM EDT, best estimate 5:44 PM. The marine layer is likely to push the effective arrival toward the later edge — if the cell weakens over the Sound, it may arrive as a wind shift with light rain rather than a full squall. Gusts ahead of the system could build to 10–15 kt WNW starting around 5 PM.

Most likely outcome for the cove: a brief sharp WNW gust front around 5:30–6:15 PM, wind shifting to NW and then dropping fast, scattered light rain rather than a heavy downpour, and clearing post-frontal by 6:30–7 PM. Surrounding inland areas — Westchester, northern Greenwich — will likely see a heavier, more intense pass.

What would change this call: a stronger cell (≥45 dBZ) developing upwind before reaching the Sound, significant marine-layer erosion, or storm motion accelerating above 45 kt — any of these would reduce the Sound's ability to moderate the convection.

arrival.blurb: Radar-tracked cell 32 mi west closing at 21 kt; the marine layer over the Sound may slow the advance or weaken it on the crossing.

wind.regime_label: Frontal transition — WNW pre-squall to NW dying post-frontal

hourly.shift_note: squall window — wind shifts NW, brief gust spike possible

wind.tactics_blurb: Pre-storm WNW favors the right side to catch the impending WNW→NW right shift on frontal passage, but the squall arriving around 5:30–6:20 PM will disrupt any racing; post-frontal NW will be 2–3 kt by 7 PM — drifting conditions and likely a shortened or abandoned series.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime C (frontal transition) — the thermal driver and the approaching front are competing, and the front is winning. Delta-T is a solid 11–12°F (Sound water 71°F, air 83°F), enough to drive a sea breeze, but only 10% of coastal stations show southerly flow and RYC is reading W 280° at 3.7 kt. The pre-frontal westerly gradient has suppressed the thermal before it could establish, and the approaching squall will finish the job. Mean authoritative station speed is 6.1 kt — light, directionally unstable, ensemble direction agreement only 0.61.

HRRR (19Z cycle) forecasts WNW 295° at 6.6 kt by 5 PM, veering to NW 307° at 4.7 kt by 7 PM as the front clears. No sea-breeze direction correction applies on Regime C — raw HRRR directions are used. There is a 30° model/obs divergence (HRRR W 274° vs observed WSW 244°), but both are describing the same physics: a light pre-frontal westerly, not a sea breeze.

The cold front squall arrives in the 5:24–6:20 PM window (see storm section). The squall itself will produce the sharpest wind of the evening — expect a brief gust front to 15–20 kt WNW ahead of the cell, followed by a hard wind shift to NW and a rapid fall. HRRR hourly averages (6–7 kt) do not capture the squall peak; actual gusts during passage may be significantly higher. After the front: NW 307–320°, dropping from ~5 kt at 7 PM to 2–3 kt by 8–9 PM.

Sunset is 8:28 PM EDT. With the thermal already suppressed and the front clearing by 7 PM, there is no evening holdover — the post-frontal NW dies well before sunset. HRRR extended outlook (fh7–12) shows a mean of 2.8 kt, confirming calm through 9 PM.

Tactics: if racing starts before 5 PM, favor the right side to be positioned for the WNW→NW right shift on frontal passage. If the start is postponed to 7–8 PM, expect 2–3 kt NW with no dominant pressure — stay central and minimize position risk. The most likely outcome tonight is a postponed or shortened series.
